title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at small museums. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Emma, The Store-Room Cataloguer
Emma volunteered at a local museum after noticing that its online collection showed only the objects on display. Most winter Saturdays she worked in a cold store-room where dusty boxes had been stacked for decades without proper shelf marks. [47]Her first important discovery was that several labels in the database described objects that were actually in different drawers.[/47] The mistake sounded small, but it meant researchers might request the wrong item. [48]Her former job as a school librarian helped her design a numbering system that staff could follow without special training.[/48] Emma says the satisfaction came slowly, because nobody applauds a tidy spreadsheet. However, she loved opening packages wrapped in newspaper from another century. She also learned not to clean anything without asking a curator first. By the end of the year, she felt she had given invisible objects a safer future and made the museum less dependent on memory.
texts.B:
### Raj, The Weekend Guide
Raj first entered the museum as a visitor during a public talk about local shipbuilding. [49]At the end of the talk, the education officer asked whether anyone would volunteer as a weekend guide, and Raj signed up before he could change his mind.[/49] He expected to repeat prepared information, but the job was less predictable. [50]He was often caught out by children who asked extremely precise questions about ropes, engines and the colour of old warning flags.[/50] That forced him to read more than the official leaflet. Raj enjoyed helping nervous visitors feel that museums were not only for experts. [55]After watching one wheelchair user miss half the harbour display, he suggested reversing the tour route so everyone could reach the main cases together.[/55] The change was simple, but staff kept it. Raj now thinks a good guide should notice the person at the back as much as the object in front.
texts.C:
### Nina, The Costume Restorer
Nina initially joined the museum because she sewed theatre costumes and wanted to practise slower, more careful work. The textile room fascinated her, especially the fragile sleeves and faded ribbons, but it also made her cautious. [51]She soon learned that some nineteenth-century fabrics could be damaged simply by spending too long under bright lights.[/51] As a result, she began timing sessions and covering pieces whenever visitors entered. She found conservation frustrating at first, because success often meant doing less than she wished. One afternoon she stitched a tiny tear in a wedding jacket that had belonged to a local fisherman. [52]When his granddaughter saw the repair, she cried because it was the first time the jacket had looked wearable in her lifetime.[/52] Nina says that moment changed how she understood old clothing. It was not decoration; it was evidence of ordinary people trying to look their best on important days.
texts.D:
### Theo, The Evening Events Helper
Theo volunteered after his grandfather’s diaries were donated to the museum, hoping the archive would help him understand stories he had heard only in fragments. [53]He became involved mainly because he wanted to understand his grandfather’s past through the museum’s records.[/53] Soon he was helping with evening events, moving chairs, welcoming speakers and checking projectors. The building looked grand from outside, but the rooms were close to flats whose residents needed quiet. [54]Theo’s most difficult responsibility was planning talks and music sessions around strict noise limits agreed with the neighbours.[/54] He sometimes felt trapped between excited performers and tired residents, yet he learned to negotiate calmly. What surprised him most was how many people stayed afterwards to share memories over tea. [56]Before leaving for university, he began writing step-by-step event notes so another volunteer could run the programme without relying on him.[/56] He still reads the diary transcripts online regularly.

questions.question: mentions discovering that museum records did not match the location of certain items?
questions.explanation: Emma found that several database labels described objects kept in different drawers.

questions.question: says a previous job helped them create an easy-to-follow organisational system?
questions.explanation: Emma used her experience as a school librarian to design a numbering system.

questions.question: became a volunteer after being invited at the end of a public event?
questions.explanation: Raj signed up after the education officer asked for volunteers after a talk.

questions.question: was challenged by young visitors asking very detailed questions?
questions.explanation: Raj was caught out by children asking precise questions about museum objects.

questions.question: learned that part of the collection could be harmed by exposure to strong lighting?
questions.explanation: Nina learned that some old fabrics could be damaged by bright lights.

questions.question: describes a repair that caused an emotional reaction from a relative of the original owner?
questions.explanation: The granddaughter cried after seeing the repaired wedding jacket.

questions.question: says a family connection motivated them to get involved?
questions.explanation: Theo volunteered to understand his grandfather’s past through the museum records.

questions.question: had to organise activities while respecting restrictions agreed with nearby residents?
questions.explanation: Theo planned events around strict noise limits agreed with neighbours.

questions.question: suggested changing the order of a visit so that disabled visitors could access the same displays?
questions.explanation: Raj proposed reversing the tour route after seeing a wheelchair user miss part of the display.

questions.question: prepared written instructions so that someone else could continue the work?
questions.explanation: Theo wrote step-by-step notes so another volunteer could run the programme.
